name: Ethiopia PHC Comprehensive CDSS — Evidence-Based Clinical Rules
sources: Ethiopian Primary Health Care Clinical Guideline (MoH 2020)
Ethiopian Standard Treatment Guidelines for General Hospitals (MoH 2020)
Ethiopian IMNCI/IMCI Integrated Management of Childhood Illness Protocol
Ethiopian National Tuberculosis, Leprosy and HIV Control Programme Guidelines (2022)
Ethiopian HIV Testing and Treatment Guideline — Test-and-Treat Protocol
Ethiopian Community Management of Acute Malnutrition (CMAM) Protocol
Ethiopian Obstetrics and Gynaecology Guideline — Maternal Danger Signs
Ethiopian NCD Prevention and Control Guideline (Hypertension, Diabetes)
Ethiopian Antimicrobial Stewardship National Action Plan
WHO Emergency and Referral Red-Flag Guidance
WHO IMNCI Chart Booklet (Adapted for Ethiopian Context)

hardStopRules.message: Critical hypoxia (SpO2 ≤90%). Give high-flow oxygen 10–15 L/min immediately. Identify underlying cause. Arrange urgent escalation or referral to hospital with oxygen therapy.
Fever with neck stiffness — bacterial meningitis until proven otherwise. Give IV/IM ceftriaxone 2g (adult) or 100mg/kg (child) as pre-referral treatment. Emergency transfer to hospital immediately.

Severe hypertension in pregnancy (BP ≥160/110) with danger signs — severe pre-eclampsia. Administer MgSO4 loading dose (4g IV over 20 min + 10g IM) and antihypertensive (nifedipine 10mg oral). Emergency obstetric referral.
Pregnancy-related vaginal bleeding — antepartum haemorrhage. Do NOT perform vaginal examination. Establish IV access, give IV fluids. Emergency obstetric referral immediately — every minute counts.

Paediatric respiratory danger signs (chest indrawing, stridor, cyanosis, lethargy, convulsions, or unable to drink). Classify as severe pneumonia or very severe febrile disease. Give pre-referral amoxicillin + oxygen. Refer urgently.
Paediatric weight is required before dosing, treatment routing, or discharge decisions can be made. Weigh the child before proceeding.
A working diagnosis must be recorded before admit or discharge decisions can be finalised.
Outstanding orders exist. Review and acknowledge all pending orders before discharging the patient.

Severe malaria danger signs detected (convulsions, impaired consciousness, inability to drink, severe vomiting, prostration, jaundice, or dark urine). IV artesunate is first-line. If IV unavailable, give rectal artesunate pre-referral. Emergency transfer.

Haemoptysis detected. Apply infection control immediately: mask patient, separate from waiting area. Urgent evaluation for TB and other causes. Refer to hospital with Xpert MTB/RIF and chest X-ray capability.
Convulsion in a pregnant patient — eclampsia until proven otherwise. Protect airway. Administer MgSO4 4g IV over 20 min + 10g IM loading dose. Emergency obstetric transfer with IV access maintained.

Postpartum haemorrhage (blood loss >500mL or clinical shock signs). Apply bimanual uterine compression. Give oxytocin 10 IU IM/IV + IV fluid resuscitation. Emergency obstetric transfer if not at CEmONC facility.
Severe Acute Malnutrition with medical complication (infection, severe anaemia, oedema, poor appetite). Refer immediately to inpatient therapeutic feeding unit (TFU/SC). Do not manage as outpatient.

Severe dehydration (lethargic or unconscious, sunken eyes, skin pinch very slow, unable to drink). Start IV Ringer's Lactate 100mL/kg immediately (Plan C). If IV not possible, refer urgently with ORS via NGT.

Neonatal danger signs present (not feeding/breastfeeding, convulsions, fast or difficult breathing, jaundice in first 24 hours, hypothermia, bulging fontanelle, or severe skin infection). Stabilise: warmth, airway, glucose. Emergency neonatal unit referral.

Hypertensive emergency (BP ≥180/120 with end-organ symptoms: chest pain, confusion, focal weakness, visual change, pulmonary oedema). Start IV labetalol or oral nifedipine 10mg. ECG. Emergency referral to hospital with ICU.

Anaphylaxis (urticaria + hypotension/airway compromise/wheeze after exposure). Administer epinephrine 0.5mg IM (adult) or 0.01mg/kg IM (child, max 0.5mg) into outer thigh immediately. Lay supine, legs elevated. IV access. Emergency transfer.

Sepsis criteria met (fever/hypothermia + tachycardia + tachypnoea with a confirmed infection source). Blood cultures if available. IV ceftriaxone 2g within 1 hour. IV fluid 30mL/kg bolus. Urgent referral to hospital.

warningRules.message: Required vital signs are missing for this checkpoint. Complete all vitals before making clinical decisions.
Discharge with pending orders acknowledged. Give patient clear return-if-worsening instructions and document follow-up plan.

Positive malaria RDT. Prescribe first-line ACT (Artemether-Lumefantrine) per weight/age dosing chart. Counsel patient on completing the full 3-day course and to return immediately if danger signs appear.

Malaria in pregnancy requires careful choice of treatment. AL is safe from the second trimester. Use quinine 600mg TDS + clindamycin 300mg TDS for 7 days in the first trimester. Confirm gestational age before prescribing.

TB screening indicated (prolonged cough plus systemic symptoms). Request sputum Xpert MTB/RIF. Apply infection control: mask patient, use separate ventilated waiting area. Document TB household contacts.

Known TB contact. Screen for active TB disease with Xpert and CXR. If active TB excluded, assess IPT eligibility (all HIV-positive contacts, and children under 5 in household) — give isoniazid 5mg/kg daily × 6 months.

Clinical features suggestive of HIV (recurrent infections, oral thrush, herpes zoster, unexplained weight loss/wasting, or generalised lymphadenopathy). Offer HIV rapid test with counselling per HCT/PITC protocol.
Patient on ART: review adherence, most recent viral load and CD4 count. Screen for TB co-infection and opportunistic infections at every visit. Refill supply to maintain uninterrupted ART.

Severe Acute Malnutrition without complications (MUAC <11.5cm, no oedema, good appetite). Eligible for Outpatient Therapeutic Programme (OTP). Dispense RUTF, de-worm, give amoxicillin prophylaxis and Vitamin A. Schedule weekly follow-up.
Moderate Acute Malnutrition (MUAC 11.5–12.5cm). Refer to Supplementary Feeding Programme (SFP). Treat any underlying illness. Monthly weight and MUAC follow-up. Nutrition counselling for caregiver.

Clinical anaemia signs (pale conjunctiva, palms, or tongue). Check haemoglobin or haematocrit. Severe anaemia (Hb <7g/dL) requires urgent management and possible transfusion. Identify and treat underlying cause.

Elevated BP in pregnancy (140–159/90–109). Perform dipstick urinalysis for proteinuria. Increase ANC monitoring frequency. Consider low-dose aspirin 75–150mg if <20 weeks. Refer promptly if BP worsens or symptoms develop.
HIV status not documented for this pregnancy. Offer HIV and syphilis test at the first ANC visit per PMTCT Option B+ protocol. If HIV-positive: initiate ART immediately regardless of CD4 count.
Iron and folic acid supplementation not documented for this pregnancy. Prescribe daily iron-folate (60mg elemental iron + 400mcg folic acid) throughout pregnancy and continuing 3 months postpartum.

Fast breathing with fever or cough — classify pneumonia per IMNCI. Treat with amoxicillin (weight-based). Reassess at 48 hours. Refer if not improving, if chest indrawing develops, or if oxygen saturation falls.

Wheeze or recurrent breathing difficulty — assess for asthma. For acute attack: salbutamol MDI 4–8 puffs + spacer every 20 min × 3 doses. Add prednisolone 1mg/kg if moderate or severe. Refer if no response after 1 hour.

Moderate dehydration with diarrhoea. Give ORS 75mL/kg over 4 hours (Plan B). Zinc 10mg/day (age <6m) or 20mg/day (age ≥6m) for 10 days in children. Continue feeding and breastfeeding. Reassess every hour.

Elevated blood pressure. Confirm on repeat reading after 15 min rest. Initiate or optimise antihypertensive (amlodipine 5mg or HCTZ 12.5–25mg as first-line). Lifestyle counselling. Follow-up in 2–4 weeks.

Mental health concern identified. Apply mhGAP assessment. Assess suicide risk — do not leave high-risk patient alone. Involve caregiver/family. Haloperidol per mhGAP for acute psychosis. Refer if beyond PHC management capacity.

Broad-spectrum antibiotic considered. Apply stewardship: confirm bacterial infection, use narrowest effective agent per Ethiopia STG, document indication and intended duration. Avoid fluoroquinolones as first-line for common URTI/community infections.

Borderline oxygen saturation (91–94%). Monitor closely. Administer supplemental oxygen if symptomatic or if SpO2 remains below 94%. Identify and address the cause. Consider referral if not improving within 30 minutes.

Elevated blood glucose or known diabetes documented. Check fasting/random glucose. Optimise glycaemic control. Screen for complications (diabetic foot, retinopathy, nephropathy). Lifestyle counselling on diet and activity.
Patient has a documented drug allergy. Review all prescribed medications against the allergy list before dispensing. Document any new adverse drug reactions encountered.
Febrile illness in a malaria-endemic area without confirmed RDT or microscopy result. Do not treat empirically with ACT without diagnostic confirmation per Ethiopia malaria elimination guidelines.

adult: Complete a full history and physical examination before prescribing. Document all findings.
Assess for the five highest-burden conditions at Ethiopian PHC: malaria, respiratory tract infection, diarrhoeal disease, tuberculosis, and HIV/AIDS — at every encounter where clinically relevant.
Record all required vital signs at each checkpoint. Blood pressure and temperature are mandatory from consult onwards.
Apply antibiotic stewardship: only prescribe antibiotics when bacterial infection is confirmed or strongly suspected. Document indication and duration.
Screen for hypertension and diabetes at every adult encounter — BP and random glucose are routine investigations.
Provide patient education on medication adherence, nutrition, and clear return-if-worsening instructions at every discharge.
Document any known drug allergies and cross-check against all prescribed medications before dispensing.
Use the standard referral pathway for any condition beyond PHC management capacity — complete the referral form with a clinical summary and pre-referral treatment given.
pediatric: Begin every paediatric encounter with the IMNCI danger sign assessment: unconscious/lethargic, convulsions, unable to drink/breastfeed, vomiting everything.
Check vaccination status at every visit and update per the Expanded Programme on Immunisation (EPI) schedule.
Measure and record weight, MUAC, and height at every encounter. Plot on the WHO growth chart.
For any child with fever: perform malaria RDT and assess respiratory rate for pneumonia classification and hydration status for dehydration classification.
Always use weight-based dosing for all paediatric medications. Confirm weight before prescribing.
For any child with IMNCI danger signs: administer pre-referral treatment and arrange immediate transfer with a written referral form.
Vitamin A supplementation: every 6 months for children 6–59 months. Document dose and date in child health card.
Screen for acute malnutrition at every visit: MUAC, bilateral pitting oedema, and conduct appetite test if SAM is suspected.
maternal: Screen for maternal danger signs at every ANC and postnatal encounter: severe headache, visual disturbance, epigastric pain, heavy vaginal bleeding, reduced fetal movement, and convulsions.
At first ANC: document HIV status, PMTCT enrolment, syphilis test result, iron-folate dispensing, blood group, and gestational age. Repeat at each subsequent visit.
Blood pressure is mandatory at every maternal encounter. Any BP ≥140/90 requires urinalysis for proteinuria and increased monitoring.
Document gestational age by LMP and fundal height at each ANC visit. Target the 8-visit ANC model per Ethiopia RMNCH guidelines.
For every delivery: apply Active Management of Third Stage of Labour (AMTSL) — oxytocin 10 IU IM, controlled cord traction, and uterine massage — to reduce PPH risk.
Refer all high-risk pregnancies to CEmONC facility in advance: previous caesarean section, twin pregnancy, severe anaemia, malpresentation, pre-eclampsia, or previous PPH history.
Postnatal care within 24–48 hours: support breastfeeding initiation, cord care, neonatal danger sign counselling, maternal complication check, and family planning counselling.
Screen all pregnant women for syphilis and offer PMTCT HIV testing. If positive: initiate ART immediately (Option B+) and plan for safe delivery and infant prophylaxis.
